title: Quiz: VQE Theory
description: Test your understanding of VQE theoretical foundations

questions.question: What does the variational principle guarantee about the expectation value <psi|H|psi> for any normalized trial state |psi>?

options.text: It equals the ground state energy E_0
It is always greater than or equal to E_0
It is always less than or equal to E_0

questions.explanation: The variational principle states that <psi|H|psi> >= E_0 for any normalized state. This upper bound property is the foundation of VQE - we search for states that minimize this expectation value.

questions.question: In VQE, what is the role of the classical optimizer?

options.text: To update the variational parameters to minimize energy
To decompose the Hamiltonian into Pauli strings

questions.explanation: The classical optimizer takes the measured energy values and updates the variational parameters theta to minimize <psi(theta)|H|psi(theta)>. This creates the hybrid quantum-classical optimization loop.

questions.question: Why must Hamiltonians be decomposed into Pauli strings for VQE?

options.text: Because quantum computers can only measure in the computational basis
Because Pauli matrices are always unitary

questions.explanation: Quantum computers measure in the computational basis. To measure <H>, we decompose H into Pauli strings, measure each term by applying appropriate basis rotations, and classically combine the results.

questions.question: What property does the Rayleigh-Ritz quotient R(|psi>) = <psi|H|psi>/<psi|psi> possess?

options.text: It is scale-invariant: R(c|psi>) = R(|psi>) for c != 0
It equals zero at the ground state

questions.explanation: The Rayleigh quotient is scale-invariant because the normalization factor |c|^2 appears in both numerator and denominator and cancels. This means we don't need to work with normalized states.

questions.question: If the VQE ansatz cannot represent the true ground state, the algorithm will still converge to the exact ground state energy.
questions.explanation: False. VQE can only find the best approximation within the space of states reachable by the ansatz. If the true ground state is outside this space, VQE will converge to a higher energy state.

questions.question: How does the statistical error in VQE energy estimation scale with the number of measurement shots N?
questions.explanation: The standard error scales as sigma/sqrt(N) where sigma is the standard deviation. This is the standard scaling for statistical estimation and means quadrupling shots halves the error.

questions.question: What determines the approximation error bound in VQE?

options.text: The number of Pauli terms in the Hamiltonian

questions.explanation: The approximation error is bounded by how well the ansatz can approximate the true ground state. An ansatz that cannot express the ground state will have a fundamental approximation error regardless of optimization.

questions.question: Which of the following are true about stationary points of the Rayleigh quotient? (Select all that apply)

options.text: They correspond to eigenstates of the Hamiltonian
The global minimum is the ground state
All stationary points have the same energy
The global maximum corresponds to the highest energy eigenstate

questions.explanation: Stationary points (where gradient = 0) are eigenstates, the global minimum is the ground state with E_0, and the global maximum is the highest eigenstate with E_max. Different stationary points have different energies corresponding to different eigenvalues.
